## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model]
**Define the die attach process and list its three primary functional roles in microsystem packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

Die attach is the semiconductor packaging operation in which a singulated silicon or MEMS die is mechanically, thermally, and sometimes electrically bonded onto a package substrate, lead frame, or carrier using a precisely dispensed bonding medium.

The three primary functional roles are:
1. **Mechanical anchoring** – providing adhesion sufficient to survive mechanical shock, vibration, and thermal cycling stresses (target shear strength > 5 MPa).
2. **Thermal conduction** – creating a low-resistance heat flow path from the device junction to the package heat sink.
3. **Electrical conduction** – in the case of conductive epoxy or solder, providing a return path for ground or signal currents.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model]
**Compare eutectic Au-Sn die attach and silver-filled epoxy die attach with respect to process temperature, thermal conductivity, and typical application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Au-Sn Eutectic | Ag-Filled Epoxy |
|---|---|---|
| Process Temperature | 280 – 320 °C | 150 – 200 °C |
| Thermal Conductivity | ~57 W/m·K | 1 – 5 W/m·K |
| Typical Application | Aerospace, hermetic, high-power RF | Consumer IC, automotive, MEMS |

The Au-Sn system provides superior thermal performance and hermeticity but at very high cost and processing temperature, while Ag-epoxy offers cost-effective, low-temperature processing suited to high-volume manufacturing.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a die attach station**, listing the role of each subsystem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A silicon die (side 8 mm × 8 mm, $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}/K$) is attached to an FR-4 substrate ($\alpha_{FR4} = 14 \times 10^{-6}/K$) using a conductive epoxy. If the assembly cools from a cure temperature of 175 °C to 25 °C, calculate the **differential strain** and comment on whether this strain is likely to cause delamination. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 Marks)**

A die attach station consists of four main subsystems:

1. **Material Handling Subsystem** – holds the wafer ring with singulated dies and the substrate magazine/leadframe reel; provides automated indexing of substrates into the bond position.
2. **Vision and Alignment Subsystem** – a high-resolution camera identifies die fiducials and substrate pads; a vision processor computes the (X, Y, θ) correction required for sub-25 µm placement accuracy.
3. **Dispense and Placement Subsystem** – the syringe pump or jet valve deposits a controlled volume of epoxy, solder paste, or sinter paste; the bondhead picks the die with a vacuum collet and places it onto the substrate.
4. **Bonding and Cure Subsystem** – the heated chuck elevates the bond stage temperature to the cure/reflow point while a force-controlled bondhead applies the required load; PID controllers regulate temperature and force throughout the cycle.

Supporting systems include a **load cell** for force feedback, a **temperature controller**, and an **in-line inspection module** (X-ray or SAM) for quality verification.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

Given:
- $L_{die} = 8$ mm
- $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}/K$
- $\alpha_{FR4} = 14 \times 10^{-6}/K$
- $T_{cure} = 175$ °C
- $T_{amb} = 25$ °C

Step 1: Compute $\Delta T$:
$$ \Delta T = 175 - 25 = 150 \text{ K} $$

Step 2: Differential strain:
$$ \varepsilon = (\alpha_{FR4} - \alpha_{Si}) \cdot \Delta T $$

$$ \varepsilon = (14 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 150 $$

$$ \varepsilon = 11.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 150 $$

$$ \varepsilon = 1710 \times 10^{-6} = 1.71 \times 10^{-3} $$

Step 3: Interpretation:
$$ \boxed{\varepsilon \approx 1.71 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{or} \;\; 0.171\%} $$

This is a **substantial strain**. With a rigid bond layer, this would likely cause **interfacial delamination** at the die corners after several thermal cycles. Therefore, a **compliant epoxy** (low shear modulus, typically < 5 GPa) is preferred over solder for large dies on FR-4.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of die attach materials** with suitable examples. Explain why nano-silver sintering is gaining importance in power electronics packaging. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A power LED die (3 mm × 3 mm) dissipates 5 W. It is attached to an aluminum PCB using a silver-sintered bondline with $k = 150$ W/m·K and BLT of 30 µm. Compute the **temperature rise across the bondline** alone. If the bondline is replaced with conventional Ag-epoxy ($k = 2$ W/m·K, same BLT), compute the **new temperature rise** and the **ratio of rise**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Die Attach Materials (7 Marks)**

Die attach materials are classified into four families:

1. **Polymer Adhesives** — e.g., epoxy resins (most common), polyimides (high temperature), silicones (low stress). Filled with Ag, Ni, or graphite particles to tune conductivity.
2. **Metallic Solders** — e.g., eutectic Au-Sn (80/20), Sn-Pb (legacy), SAC (lead-free). Provide high thermal and electrical conductivity.
3. **Sintered Materials** — e.g., nano-silver paste sintered at 250 °C under pressure. Offer very high $k$ (> 150 W/m·K) and high remelt temperature.
4. **Glass and Ceramic Systems** — e.g., Ag-glass cements used in hermetic military packages.

**Why nano-Ag sintering is gaining importance in power electronics:**
- Very high thermal conductivity (**150–250 W/m·K**, surpassing even Au-Sn).
- High melting/sintering temperature allows operation near **300 °C junction**.
- Lead-free and RoHS-compliant.
- Low void content due to pressure-assisted sintering.
- Enables higher power density in EV inverters and 5G RF amplifiers.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

Given:
- Die area $A = (3 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
- $Q = 5$ W
- $BLT = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m

**Step 1: Sintered Ag case ($k_1 = 150$ W/m·K)**

$$ R_{th,1} = \frac{BLT}{k_1 \cdot A} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-6}}{150 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ R_{th,1} = \frac{30}{1350} = 0.0222 \text{ K/W} $$

$$ \Delta T_1 = Q \cdot R_{th,1} = 5 \times 0.0222 = 0.111 \text{ K} $$

**Step 2: Epoxy case ($k_2 = 2$ W/m·K)**

$$ R_{th,2} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{30}{18} = 1.667 \text{ K/W} $$

$$ \Delta T_2 = 5 \times 1.667 = 8.33 \text{ K} $$

**Step 3: Ratio**

$$ \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} = \frac{8.33}{0.111} = \boxed{75 \times} $$

**Comment:** The same bondline, with the same geometry, causes a **75× higher** temperature rise when conventional epoxy replaces silver sinter. For high-power LEDs, this difference is the difference between reliable operation and thermal runaway. This is precisely why **nano-Ag sintering** has become the gold standard for **EV power modules** and **high-brightness LEDs**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to convert mm and µm to meters before plugging into the thermal resistance formula. Always express all dimensions in SI units.
> 2. **Confusing k (thermal conductivity) with κ (or using the wrong value).** Board exams use **W/m·K** consistently — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Sign convention in CTE mismatch:** Always use $|\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}|$, not the negative value. The strain magnitude, not direction, drives cracking.
> 4. **Confusing "process temperature" with "operating temperature."** Die attach cure is a one-time thermal event; operating temperature affects reliability differently.
> 5. **Forgetting the 7-mark sub-part structure:** Examiners expect **diagram + explanation** for part (a) and **formula + substitution + boxed answer + interpretation** for part (b).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Die attach = mechanical + thermal + electrical** triple-function bonding step.
- **Five material classes:** polymer adhesives, solders, sintered metals, glass-ceramics, and conductive epoxies.
- **Key process parameters:** placement accuracy (**±5 µm**), BLT (**25–50 µm**), void % (**< 2 %**), cure temperature (**150–350 °C**), bond force (**0.5–5 N**).
- **Thermal resistance formula:** $R_{th} = \dfrac{BLT}{k \cdot A}$ — derive once, use repeatedly.
- **CTE mismatch** drives long-term fatigue; **compliance** in the attach layer extends reliability.
- **Eutectic Au-Sn:** 80/20 composition, **280 °C** melting — aerospace standard.
- **Nano-Ag sintering:** delivers **k > 150 W/m·K** — modern high-power standard.
- **Silver-filled epoxy:** the *workhorse* material for > 80 % of consumer IC packaging.
- **Quality inspection:** X-ray for voids, SAM for delamination, AOI for placement.
- **Always box your final numerical answer** in KTU exams to draw the examiner's eye.
- **For MEMS-specific die attach:** use low-modulus, low-outgassing adhesives; avoid contamination of moving structures; consider cavity packages for optical/sensing devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solder Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Solder Alloy # Solder Alloy — Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **solder alloy** is a fusible metal alloy, typically with a melting point below **450 °C**, used to create a permanent metallic bond between two or more workpieces in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) and microelectronic packaging. The bond is formed by melting the alloy so that it wets the joining surfaces and subsequently solidifies, producing both an **electrical interconnection** and a **mechanical/thermal joint**. In the context of **MEMS packaging and assembly**, solder alloys fulfil four critical roles: 1. **Die attach** — mechanical and thermal attachment of the MEMS die to the package substrate. 2. **Flip-chip interconnect** — formation of solder bumps for chip-to-substrate electrical connection. 3. **Hermetic lid sealing** — sealing of the package cavity using solder ring/frame techniques. 4. **Heat dissipation path** — providing a low thermal resistance path from the active die to the package. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Solder alloys are classified as *bonding materials* in MEMS assembly, alongside *anodic bonding glasses*, *eutectic bonding alloys* (e.g., Au-Si, Au-Sn), and *adhesive bonding polymers*. Soldering is the **most widely used** low-cost joining technique in commercial MEMS production. ## Common Solder Alloy Families | Family | Typical Composition | Melting Point (°C) | Status | |---|---|---|---| | Tin-Lead (Sn-Pb) | 63Sn / 37Pb (eutectic) | **183** | Legacy / restricted (RoHS) | | Tin-Silver-Copper (SAC) | 96.5Sn / 3.0Ag / 0.5Cu (SAC305) | **217** | Industry standard lead-free | | Tin-Silver (Sn-Ag) | 96.5Sn / 3.5Ag | **221** | High reliability | | Tin-Antimony (Sn-Sb) | 95Sn / 5Sb | **240** | High-temperature | | Gold-Tin (Au-Sn) | 80Au / 20Sn (eutectic) | **280** | Hermetic / opto-MEMS | | Indium-based | 52In / 48Sn | **118** | Low-temperature / cryogenic | ## Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Glue of Microelectronics" Think of solder alloy as a **microscopic metallic glue gun stick** that has been engineered to melt at a precise, controlled temperature. When you heat the joint, the solder "melts" like butter on a hot pan, flows over the metallic surfaces to be joined (much like water spreads on a clean glass surface), and then **solidifies** upon cooling, locking the components together both **electrically and mechanically**. The genius of MEMS solder engineering lies in the fact that the melting temperature is *lower* than the temperatures that would damage the silicon die (typically rated to ~260 °C for short durations), making it a *gentle but strong* joining method for temperature-sensitive microstructures. > [!NOTE] > The **wetting** behaviour of molten solder is governed by the contact angle **θ** with the metal pad. A contact angle **θ < 90°** indicates *good wetting* (hydrophilic-like behaviour in metallic systems), while **θ > 90°** indicates *poor wetting* (the solder "balls up" rather than spreading). This is the single most important visual quality indicator in solder joint inspection. ## Why Solder Alloys Matter in MEMS - **CTE matching:** Solders can be formulated to have coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) intermediate between silicon (~2.6 ppm/°C) and the package substrate (Al₂O₃ ~6–7 ppm/°C or FR-4 ~14–18 ppm/°C), reducing thermomechanical stress. - **Self-alignment:** Surface tension of molten solder during reflow *automatically aligns* a flip-chip die to the substrate pads with sub-micron precision — a critical feature for MEMS optical and inertial devices. - **Fatigue tolerance:** Soft solder alloys (especially SAC) accommodate thermal cycling stress through *plastic deformation* of the joint, extending device life. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Wetting Thermodynamics — The Young–Dupré Equation Wetting is the **fundamental prerequisite** for a reliable solder joint. It is quantified by the **contact angle (θ)** of the molten solder droplet on a solid metal pad, governed by the equilibrium of interfacial energies at the three-phase (solid–liquid–gas) boundary. ### Young's Equation (1805) $$\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$$ Where: - $\gamma_{SG}$ = solid–gas interfacial energy (mJ/m²) - $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy (mJ/m²) - $\gamma_{LG}$ = liquid–gas interfacial energy (mJ/m²) - $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle (degrees) ### Work of Adhesion (Dupré Extension) $$W_a = \gamma_{SG} + \gamma_{LG} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta)$$ This represents the energy released per unit area when the liquid–gas interface replaces the solid–liquid interface. **Larger $W_a$ → better bonding**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Key Point:** Flux is applied to the joint area prior to reflow to *reduce* $\gamma_{SL}$ by dissolving native oxides, thereby *increasing* $W_a$ and *decreasing* θ. Without flux, the molten solder cannot wet an oxidized copper pad. ## 2.2 Eutectic Behaviour & Phase-Diagram Fundamentals A **eutectic alloy** melts and solidifies at a *single, sharp temperature* (the eutectic point), producing a fine, uniform microstructure. This is *highly desirable* in packaging because: - **No plastic (mushy) range** → no risk of joint disturbance during cooling. - **Lower melting point** than either constituent → process-friendly. - **Reproducible joint morphology** → high manufacturing yield. For the **Sn-Pb system**, the eutectic point occurs at **61.9 wt% Sn / 38.1 wt% Pb, Tₑ = 183 °C**. For the **SAC system**, the near-eutectic composition is **Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu with Tₑ ≈ 217 °C**. ## 2.3 Solder Bump Geometry for Flip-Chip A standard **cylindrical solder bump** is described by its diameter $d$, height $h$, and pad pitch $p$. The joint volume and stand-off height determine both **electrical performance** and **mechanical reliability**. | Parameter | Formula | Units | |---|---|---| | Bump volume | $V = \pi d^2 h / 4$ | m³ | | Cross-sectional area | $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ | m² | | Bump aspect ratio | $\text{AR} = h / d$ | dimensionless | | Pad pitch (typical) | $p \geq 1.5 d$ | m | | Electrical resistance | $R = \rho_{solder} \cdot h / A$ | Ω | | Current carrying capacity | $I = J_{max} \cdot A$ | A | Where $\rho_{solder}$ is the bulk electrical resistivity of the alloy (e.g., **~15 µΩ·cm for SAC305**, ~15 µΩ·cm for eutectic Sn-Pb) and $J_{max}$ is the maximum permissible current density (typically **$10^4$–$10^5$ A/cm²** for steady-state operation; electromigration limits kick in above $\sim 10^5$ A/cm²). ## 2.4 Thermomechanical Stress in a Solder Joint The dominant failure mode of a flip-chip solder joint is **thermomechanical fatigue** caused by CTE mismatch between the die, the solder, and the substrate under thermal cycling. ### Shear Strain due to CTE Mismatch $$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}$$ The differential thermal displacement at the joint interface is: $$\delta = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$ Where $L$ is the distance from the **neutral point (NP)** of the die to the bump. The resulting **shear strain** in a cylindrical bump of height $h$ is: $$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{h} = \frac{\Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{h}$$ The corresponding **shear stress** (in the elastic regime) is: $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = \frac{G \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{h}$$ Where $G$ is the shear modulus of the solder alloy at the operating temperature. > [!NOTE] > **Design Heuristic:** Placing bumps *as close as possible* to the neutral point **L ≈ 0** dramatically reduces shear strain. The **"underfill"** encapsulant surrounding the bumps is used in area-array packages to constrain shear deformation and extend fatigue life by 10×–100×. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Equation | Key Variables | Typical Values | |---|---|---|---| | Contact angle (wetting) | $\cos\theta = (\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}) / \gamma_{LG}$ | Interfacial energies | θ < 90° for good wetting | | Work of adhesion | $W_a = \gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Liquid-gas energy, θ | — | | Bump volume | $V = \pi d^2 h / 4$ | d = diameter, h = height | Typical: d = 100 µm, h = 70 µm | | Bump resistance | $R = \rho h / A$ | ρ, h, A | ~1–10 mΩ per bump | | CTE-mismatch shear strain | $\gamma = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L / h$ | Δα, ΔT, L, h | — | | CTE-mismatch shear stress | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | G, γ | τᵧ ≈ 30–40 MPa (SAC) | | Coffin–Manson fatigue life | $N_f = A \cdot (\Delta\gamma)^{-n}$ | A, n = fatigue ductility | n ≈ 1.5–2.0 for SAC | | Arrhenius creep rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$ | A, n, Q, R, T | Q ≈ 60–100 kJ/mol (SAC) | | Sn-Pb eutectic | 63Sn-37Pb | Composition | Tₘ = 183 °C | | SAC305 eutectic | Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu | Composition | Tₘ = 217 °C | | Au-Sn eutectic | 80Au-20Sn | Composition | Tₘ = 280 °C | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Consumer MEMS** (accelerometers, gyroscopes in smartphones): SAC305 flip-chip + underfill. - **Automotive MEMS** (under-hood pressure sensors): High-Pb or Au-Sn for high-temperature reliability. - **Opto-MEMS / MOEMS** (DLP, micromirror arrays): Au-Sn for hermeticity and optical-grade flatness. - **Medical MEMS** (implantable pressure sensors): Biocompatible Au-Sn or Pt-based solders. - **RF MEMS** (switches, filters): Low-loss Sn-Pb or low-voiding SAC with vacuum reflow. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **RoHS Directive (2002/95/EC)** and its recast **RoHS 2 (2011/65/EU)** restrict the use of lead (Pb) in most commercial electronic assemblies, making **lead-free SAC alloys the de facto industry standard**. However, exemptions exist for high-reliability sectors (military, aerospace, implantable medical) where **Pb-rich solders (e.g., 95Pb-5Sn, 92.5Pb-5Sn-2.5Ag)** remain in use due to their superior thermomechanical fatigue resistance and lack of tin-whisker growth. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** KTU 14-mark questions on solder alloys frequently test the **CTE-mismatch shear strain derivation** and the **Coffin–Manson fatigue equation**, both of which are derived below in full, step-by-step algebraic detail. ## Derivation 1 — Shear Strain in a Flip-Chip Solder Bump under Thermal Load ### Problem Statement A silicon die ($\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \text{ ppm/°C}$) of side length $15 \text{ mm}$ is flip-chip bonded to an alumina substrate ($\alpha_{Al_2O_3} = 7.0 \text{ ppm/°C}$) using SAC305 solder bumps of height $h = 70 \text{ µm}$ and diameter $d = 100 \text{ µm}$. The die is subjected to a thermal excursion $\Delta T = 125 \text{ °C}$ (e.g., from $-25$ °C to $+100$ °C). Determine the shear strain and shear stress in the *corner-most* bump, which is located at distance $L = 10.6 \text{ mm}$ from the neutral point of the die. Take $G_{SAC305} = 16 \text{ GPa}$ at 100 °C. ### Step 1 — Compute the Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) Mismatch The differential thermal expansion coefficient between the substrate and the die is: $$\Delta\alpha = \alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die} = 7.0 \text{ ppm/°C} - 2.6 \text{ ppm/°C} = 4.4 \text{ ppm/°C}$$ Converting to SI units (per °C): $$\Delta\alpha = 4.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ °C}^{-1}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Differential Thermal Displacement at the Bump Location The in-plane thermal displacement of the die edge relative to the substrate at distance $L$ from the neutral point is: $$\delta = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$ $$\delta = (4.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ °C}^{-1}) \times (125 \text{ °C}) \times (10.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m})$$ Computing the numerical product step by step: $$\delta = 4.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 125 \times 10.6 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\delta = (4.4 \times 125) \times 10^{-6} \times 10.6 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\delta = 550 \times 10^{-6} \times 10.6 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\delta = 550 \times 10.6 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\delta = 5830 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 5.83 \text{ µm}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Shear Strain in the Solder Bump The shear strain $\gamma$ in the bump is defined as the ratio of lateral displacement to bump height (small-angle approximation): $$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{h} = \frac{5.83 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}}{70 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}}$$ $$\gamma = 0.0833 \text{ (dimensionless)} = 8.33 \text{ \%}$$ This is a *very large* strain — well beyond the elastic limit of the solder — confirming that the bump will deform plastically each thermal cycle, leading to **fatigue failure**. ### Step 4 — Compute the Elastic Shear Stress (Upper Bound) Using the shear modulus $G = 16 \text{ GPa}$ at the operating temperature: $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = (16 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}) \times 0.0833$$ $$\tau = 1.333 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 1.33 \text{ GPa}$$ ### Step 5 — Compare to the Yield Strength of SAC305 The yield strength of SAC305 in shear is approximately $\tau_y \approx 30$ MPa. Since the elastic stress (1.33 GPa) far exceeds $\tau_y$, the bump yields immediately upon the first thermal cycle. The actual stress sustained in service is therefore **clamped at $\tau_y \approx 30$ MPa**, and the *inelastic* strain increment per cycle is: $$\Delta\gamma_{inelastic} = \gamma_{total} - \frac{\tau_y}{G} = 0.0833 - \frac{30 \times 10^6}{16 \times 10^9} = 0.0833 - 0.00188 = 0.0814$$ ### Step 6 — Predict Fatigue Life Using the Coffin–Manson Relation The Coffin–Manson low-cycle fatigue relation is: $$N_f = A \cdot (\Delta\gamma_{inelastic})^{-n}$$ For SAC305, empirically determined constants are $A = 1.5$ and $n = 1.9$ (typical for eutectic Sn-rich solders in shear). Substituting: $$N_f = 1.5 \times (0.0814)^{-1.9}$$ Computing the exponent first: $$0.0814^{-1.9} = e^{-1.9 \cdot \ln(0.0814)} = e^{-1.9 \times (-2.508)} = e^{4.765} = 116.7$$ Therefore: $$N_f = 1.5 \times 116.7 \approx 175 \text{ cycles}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Caution:** The *bare* (no underfill) flip-chip joint in this example fails in fewer than 200 thermal cycles. In practice, **underfill encapsulation** redistributes the shear strain across the entire chip–substrate interface, extending $N_f$ to **1000–3000 cycles**. Without underfill, the answer above is physically realistic but practically catastrophic. --- ## Derivation 2 — Solder Bump Electrical Resistance for a Current Carrying Joint ### Problem Statement A SAC305 solder bump of diameter $d = 100 \text{ µm}$ and height $h = 70 \text{ µm}$ carries a steady current of $I = 0.5 \text{ A}$. Compute its DC electrical resistance, voltage drop, and the steady-state current density. Use $\rho_{SAC305} = 15.0 \text{ µΩ·cm}$. ### Step 1 — Convert Resistivity to SI Units $$\rho = 15.0 \text{ µΩ·cm} = 15.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Ω·cm} = 15.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Ω·m} = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Ω·m}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Area of the Bump $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (100 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4}$$ $$A = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-8}}{4} = 0.7854 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Bump Resistance $$R = \frac{\rho \cdot h}{A} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (70 \times 10^{-6})}{7.854 \times 10^{-9}}$$ Numerator: $$1.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 70 \times 10^{-6} = 105 \times 10^{-13} = 1.05 \times 10^{-11} \text{ Ω·m}^2$$ Dividing by the area: $$R = \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-11}}{7.854 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.337 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ω} = 1.34 \text{ mΩ}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Voltage Drop $$V = I \cdot R = 0.5 \text{ A} \times 1.337 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ω} = 6.69 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.669 \text{ mV}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute the Current Density $$J = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{0.5 \text{ A}}{7.854 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2} = 6.37 \times 10^{7} \text{ A/m}^2 = 6.37 \times 10^{3} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ This is well within the safe operating envelope (typically < 10⁴ A/cm² for steady state) and far below the electromigration threshold. ### Step 6 — Power Dissipation in the Bump $$P = I^2 R = (0.5)^2 \times 1.337 \times 10^{-3} = 3.34 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 334 \text{ µW}$$ This heat must be removed by the package thermal path, contributing to the *junction-to-ambient* thermal resistance budget. --- ## Derivation 3 — Wetting Force on a Solder Pad (Capillary Model) The **wetting force** drives the *self-alignment* of a flip-chip die during reflow. For a molten solder bump bridging a chip pad to a substrate pad separated by gap $h$, the capillary force is: $$F_{capillary} = \gamma_{LG} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \pi d$$ For SAC305 at 250 °C, $\gamma_{LG} \approx 500 \text{ mN/m}$ and $\cos\theta \approx 0.7$ (good wetting on Cu-OSP pad): $$F_{capillary} = (500 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}) \times 0.7 \times \pi \times (100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m})$$ $$F_{capillary} = 0.5 \times 0.7 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = 1.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N} = 110 \text{ µN per bump}$$ For a die with 200 peripheral bumps, the *total* self-alignment force is: $$F_{total} = 200 \times 110 \text{ µN} = 22 \text{ mN}$$ This is the force that drags the chip into sub-micron alignment with the substrate pads during the reflow cool-down — a remarkable feature of solder-based flip-chip assembly. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Figure 1 — Solder Flip-Chip Assembly — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DIE["MEMS / Silicon Die"] DIE1[Active MEMS Structure
Accelerometer / Gyroscope / Pressure Sensor] DIE2[BEOL Metal Pads
Al or Cu with UBM] DIE3[Solder Bump UBM
Under Bump Metallization Layer] end subgraph JOINT["Solder Interconnect Region"] JB1[Reflowed SAC305 Bump
Diameter 100 um Height 70 um] JB2[IMC Layer
Cu6Sn5 / Cu3Sn Intermetallics] JB3[Flux Residue
Post-Reflow Cleaning Required] end subgraph SUB["Package Substrate"] SUB1[Cu Trace on Substrate
OSP or ENIG Finish] SUB2[Dielectric Layer
FR4 or Al2O3 or LTCC] SUB3[Thermal Via Array
Heat Dissipation Path] end subgraph UNDER["Encapsulation"] UND1[Underfill Resin
Epoxy with Silica Filler] UND2[CTE 25 to 30 ppm per C
Stress Buffer] end DIE1 --> DIE2 DIE2 --> DIE3 DIE3 --> JB1 JB1 --> JB2 JB2 --> SUB1 SUB1 --> SUB2 SUB2 --> SUB3 JB1 -.-> JB3 JB1 --> UND1 UND1 --> UND2 UND2 --> SUB1 ``` ## Figure 2 — Solder Reflow Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step 1: Flux Application
No-Clean or Water-Soluble] --> B[Step 2: Pre-Heat Zone
150 to 180 C Ramp 1 to 3 C per sec] B --> C[Step 3: Soak Zone
150 to 200 C 60 to 120 sec
Flux Activation] C --> D[Step 4: Reflow Zone
Peak 240 to 260 C
Above Liquidus 217 C for SAC] D --> E[Step 5: Cooling Zone
Controlled 2 to 4 C per sec
Fine Grain Formation] E --> F[Step 6: Solidified Joint
IMC Layer Growth Begins] F --> G[Step 7: Inspection
X-Ray SAM Optical] G --> H[Step 8: Underfill Dispense
Capillary Flow] H --> I[Step 9: Underfill Cure
150 C for 30 min Typical] I --> J[Step 10: Final Test] ``` ## Figure 3 — Solder Bump Failure Mechanism Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Solder Bump Failure Modes] --> B[Mechanical] A --> C[Electrical] A --> D[Chemical] A --> E[Thermomechanical] B --> B1[Shear Fracture
Ductile vs Brittle] B --> B2[Pad Cratering
Substrate Failure] B --> B3[Electromigration
Hillock / Voids] C --> C1[Increased Resistance
IMC Growth] C --> C2[Open Circuit
Joint Fracture] C --> C3[Short Circuit
Solder Bridge] D --> D1[Corrosion
Flux Residue Attack] D --> D2[Whisker Growth
Sn-Pb Replacement Issue] D --> D3[Oxidation
Storage Time Effects] E --> E1[Thermal Fatigue
Coffin-Manson Failure] E --> E2[Creep Deformation
High Temp Operation] E --> E3[CTE Mismatch Stress
Corner Bump Critical] ``` ## Figure 4 — Cross-Section of a Hermetic Solder-Sealed MEMS Package ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PKG["Hermetic MEMS Package"] LID[Lid Kovar or Ceramic
Plated with Au-Sn] RING[Solder Seal Ring
Au-Sn 80 20 Eutectic
Melting Point 280 C] SUBSTRATE[Substrate Al2O3 or Si
with Au Plated Ring] DIE[Silicon MEMS Die
with Cavity if Needed] CAV[Cavity
Sealed Atmosphere N2 or Vacuum] end LID --> RING RING --> SUBSTRATE DIE --> SUBSTRATE LID --- CAV CAV --- DIE ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Figure 4 Explanation:** The Au-Sn eutectic solder ring is the *industry standard* for **hermetic lid sealing** of MEMS packages because (i) it has a sharp eutectic melting point at 280 °C — well above standard SAC reflow, enabling *sequential* die-attach then lid-seal process flows; (ii) it forms a *fluxless* bond (no residue to outgas and contaminate the cavity); and (iii) Au-Sn is *resistant to creep* and provides long-term hermeticity (helium leak rate < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s). # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define solder alloy. List any four important properties required for a solder alloy to be used in MEMS packaging.** **Model Answer:** A solder alloy is a fusible metal alloy with a melting point below 450 °C, used to form a permanent metallic bond between components in MEMS assembly by melting, wetting, and re-solidifying the material at the joint interface. Four important properties: 1. **Appropriate melting point** — below the thermal budget of the MEMS die (typically < 260 °C for short exposures), yet high enough to survive subsequent assembly steps. 2. **Good wettability** — low contact angle (θ < 90°) on the metallized pad, indicating strong interfacial adhesion and reliable joint formation. 3. **Adequate mechanical strength** — sufficient shear strength (typically 20–60 MPa for SAC alloys) and ductility to accommodate CTE-mismatch stress under thermal cycling. 4. **Good electrical and thermal conductivity** — to provide low-resistance electrical interconnect and efficient heat removal from the active die. 5. *(Any other valid property: corrosion resistance, fatigue resistance, compatibility with flux, RoHS compliance)*. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the significance of using SAC305 (Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu) as a lead-free solder alloy in MEMS flip-chip packaging. Mention its melting point.** **Model Answer:** SAC305 has become the *industry standard* lead-free solder alloy in MEMS and microelectronic packaging due to the following reasons: 1. **RoHS compliance** — Lead-free, satisfying the Restriction of Hazardous Substances Directive that mandates the elimination of Pb from commercial electronic assemblies. 2. **Near-eutectic behaviour** — The composition 96.5Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu is close to the ternary eutectic, producing a sharp melting transition. 3. **Melting point of 217 °C** — Sufficiently high to provide thermal margin, yet well within the safe processing window of silicon-based MEMS devices. 4. **Good wettability** — Excellent wetting on Cu, Ni/Au, and OSP (Organic Solderability Preservative) finished pads. 5. **Superior mechanical properties** — Formation of finely dispersed Cu₆Sn₅ and Ag₃Sn intermetallic particles provides dispersion strengthening, improving creep resistance and thermal fatigue life compared to binary Sn-Ag or Sn-Cu systems. 6. **Compatibility with existing reflow profiles** — Can be processed on standard SAC reflow equipment with peak temperatures of 240–260 °C. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Question] **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **flip-chip solder bump assembly process** and explain the **self-alignment phenomenon** during reflow. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **shear strain** experienced by a peripheral flip-chip solder bump due to CTE mismatch between the die and the substrate under thermal cycling. A silicon die of size 10 mm × 10 mm is bonded to an FR-4 substrate using SAC305 solder bumps of height 75 µm. The bump is located at distance 6.5 mm from the die neutral point. The die is subjected to a thermal cycle of $\Delta T = 100 \text{ °C}$. Given $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \text{ ppm/°C}$ and $\alpha_{FR-4} = 16 \text{ ppm/°C}$, compute the **shear strain in the bump**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) **Flip-chip solder bump assembly process:** 1. **Wafer-level UBM (Under Bump Metallization) deposition** — A multi-layer metallization stack (typically sputtered Ti/Cu or electroplated Ni/Au) is deposited on the die bond pads to provide adhesion, diffusion barrier, and a wettable surface. 2. **Solder deposition** — Solder is deposited on the UBM by **electroplating** (most common for fine-pitch), **screen printing of solder paste**, or **solder ball placement / solder jetting**. 3. **Reflow** — The wafer is heated above the solder liquidus temperature (e.g., 240–260 °C for SAC305). The solder melts, wets the UBM and the corresponding substrate pad, and forms a truncated-spherical bump. 4. **Wafer singulation** — The wafer is diced into individual chips. 5. **Flip-chip placement** — The die is flipped and the bumps are aligned to the substrate pads using a pick-and-place tool with ~5–10 µm placement accuracy. 6. **Second reflow (chip-level)** — The assembly is heated above the solder liquidus. Surface tension of the molten solder **self-aligns** the die to the substrate with sub-micron precision. 7. **Underfill dispensing and curing** — A filled epoxy is dispensed along the die edge and drawn into the gap by capillary action, then thermally cured to provide mechanical reinforcement. **Self-alignment phenomenon:** When the solder melts, the molten column is bounded by the chip pad and the substrate pad. The molten solder has two principal driving forces: - **Gravity** (negligible at the micro-scale due to high surface-to-volume ratio of the bump). - **Surface tension** of the liquid solder ($\gamma_{LG}$), which acts to *minimize* the surface area of the molten bump. If the chip is laterally offset from the substrate pad, the molten solder column is *asymmetric* and its liquid–gas interface is not at the minimum-area configuration. The lateral component of the surface tension vector pulls the chip back into alignment. The alignment is automatic because any lateral offset *increases* the surface energy of the system, and the system relaxes to the *minimum-energy* (perfectly aligned) configuration. Final alignment accuracy is typically **±1–2 µm**, far better than the ±5–10 µm placement accuracy of the pick-and-place tool. #### Model Solution for (b) **Derivation of Shear Strain:** Let $\alpha_{die}$ and $\alpha_{sub}$ be the coefficients of thermal expansion of the die and substrate respectively, and let $L$ be the distance of a given bump from the neutral point of the die. The lateral thermal expansion of the die surface at distance $L$ is: $$\delta_{die} = \alpha_{die} \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$ The corresponding thermal expansion of the substrate is: $$\delta_{sub} = \alpha_{sub} \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$ The *differential* displacement that must be accommodated by the solder bump is: $$\delta = \delta_{sub} - \delta_{die} = (\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$ The shear strain in the bump (defined as the ratio of lateral displacement to bump height, for small deformations) is: $$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{h} = \frac{\Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{h}$$ **Numerical Computation:** $$\Delta\alpha = \alpha_{FR-4} - \alpha_{Si} = 16 - 2.6 = 13.4 \text{ ppm/°C} = 13.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ °C}^{-1}$$ $$\gamma = \frac{(13.4 \times 10^{-6}) \times (100) \times (6.5 \times 10^{-3})}{75 \times 10^{-6}}$$ Numerator: $$13.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 1.34 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$1.34 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.5 \times 10^{-3} = 8.71 \times 10^{-6}$$ Dividing by the denominator: $$\gamma = \frac{8.71 \times 10^{-6}}{75 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{8.71}{75} = 0.1161$$ $$\boxed{\gamma = 0.116 \text{ (dimensionless)} = 11.6\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Sign convention errors** — Always take $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}$. Reversing this sign will produce a negative strain and *lose 1 mark*. > 2. **Unit conversion errors** — Failure to convert mm → m and µm → m *consistently* is the single most common mistake in this derivation. Show all unit conversions explicitly in the answer script. > 3. **Forgetting to state the assumption** that the strain is uniform across the bump height. In a real joint, strain is concentrated near the interfaces, but the *average* engineering strain $\gamma = \delta/h$ is the KTU-accepted approximation. > 4. **Not interpreting the result** — A shear strain of 11.6% is far above the elastic limit, confirming the bump will *deform plastically* per cycle. State this explicitly for full marks. --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Question – Alternative Choice] **(a)** Explain the **Young–Dupré equation** for solder wetting on a metal pad and describe how the contact angle is used as a **quality indicator** in solder joint inspection. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A eutectic **63Sn-37Pb solder bump** of diameter 120 µm and height 80 µm is used in a flip-chip assembly. The bulk resistivity of the solder is $\rho = 14.5 \text{ µΩ·cm}$. If the bump carries a current of 0.4 A, compute the (i) bump resistance, (ii) voltage drop, and (iii) current density. Comment on whether the bump is operating within safe electromigration limits. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) **Young–Dupré Equation:** The equilibrium of a liquid solder droplet on a solid metal pad is governed by the balance of three interfacial energies at the three-phase (solid–liquid–vapour) contact line: $$\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$$ Solving for the contact angle: $$\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}$$ The **Young–Dupré work of adhesion** is the energy released per unit area when the liquid–vapour interface is replaced by a solid–liquid interface: $$W_a = \gamma_{SG} + \gamma_{LG} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta)$$ **Interpretation:** - $\theta \to 0°$ (complete wetting): The solder spreads as a thin film; $W_a = 2\gamma_{LG}$ is maximum. - $\theta = 90°$: The boundary between good and poor wetting; $W_a = \gamma_{LG}$. - $\theta \to 180°$ (no wetting): The solder beads up like mercury on glass; $W_a \to 0$. **Contact Angle as Quality Indicator:** In a quality-controlled reflow process, a sample of joints is cross-sectioned or examined by a *sessile-drop* goniometer to measure $\theta$. A contact angle of $\theta < 20°$ on a clean Cu-OSP pad is considered *excellent wetting*; $\theta < 35°$ is *acceptable*; $\theta > 45°$ indicates *de-wetting* and is rejected. Factors that increase $\theta$ include surface oxidation, insufficient flux activity, excessive heating rate, and contamination of the pad surface. **Flux action:** Flux chemically reduces the surface oxides on the metal pad, lowering $\gamma_{SL}$ and thereby *reducing* θ. This is why flux is indispensable for achieving reliable solder joints. #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** $d = 120 \text{ µm} = 120 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $h = 80 \text{ µm} = 80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $\rho = 14.5 \text{ µΩ·cm} = 14.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Ω·m} = 1.45 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Ω·m}$, $I = 0.4 \text{ A}$. **(i) Bump Resistance:** Cross-sectional area: $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (120 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10^{-8}}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$ Resistance: $$R = \frac{\rho h}{A} = \frac{(1.45 \times 10^{-7}) \times (80 \times 10^{-6})}{1.131 \times 10^{-8}}$$ Numerator: $$1.45 \times 10^{-7} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} = 116 \times 10^{-13} = 1.16 \times 10^{-11}$$ $$R = \frac{1.16 \times 10^{-11}}{1.131 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.026 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ω} \approx 1.03 \text{ mΩ}$$ **(ii) Voltage Drop:** $$V = I \times R = 0.4 \times 1.026 \times 10^{-3} = 4.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.41 \text{ mV}$$ **(iii) Current Density:** $$J = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{0.4}{1.131 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.54 \times 10^{7} \text{ A/m}^2 = 3.54 \times 10^{3} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 3.54 \text{ kA/cm}^2$$ **Comment on Electromigration Safety:** The general safe steady-state limit for solder electromigration is $J_{max} \approx 10^4 \text{ A/cm}^2$ (with conservative design) to $10^5 \text{ A/cm}^2$ (at the onset of measurable mass transport). Our computed $J = 3.54 \text{ kA/cm}^2$ is **well within the safe operating regime**, with roughly a factor of 3 margin below the conservative design limit. Therefore, the bump is operating safely with respect to electromigration. However, the *total* current carried by 100 such bumps simultaneously would be 40 A, and the cumulative joule heating (40 A × 1.03 mΩ × 0.4 A = ~16 mW per bump) must be considered in the package thermal design. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (b):** > 1. Students frequently forget to convert *both* µm → m *and* µΩ·cm → Ω·m. Use a single systematic unit conversion table before substitution. (Penalty: 2 marks) > 2. Students often confuse $A$ (cross-sectional area, m²) with $A$ (ampere, current unit) in the same expression. Use different notation such as $A_c$ for area. (Penalty: 1 mark) > 3. Failing to **comment** on the result is a common 1-mark deduction. The numerical answer without engineering interpretation is incomplete. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - **Solder alloy = fusible metal alloy, Tₘ < 450 °C**, used for electrical + mechanical + thermal joints in MEMS. - **Primary families:** Sn-Pb (63/37, Tₘ = 183 °C, legacy/RoHS-restricted), **SAC305 (Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu, Tₘ = 217 °C, industry standard lead-free)**, Au-Sn (80/20, Tₘ = 280 °C, hermetic sealing), high-Pb (Pb-rich, high-reliability aerospace/medical). - **Young's equation:** $\cos\theta = (\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}) / \gamma_{LG}$. Good wetting ⇒ θ < 90° (ideally < 20° for production). - **Work of adhesion (Dupré):** $W_a = \gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta)$. Higher $W_a$ ⇒ stronger interfacial bond. - **Eutectic** = sharp single melting point (no pasty range) — preferred for repeatable joint morphology. - **Flip-chip bumps:** cylindrical, $V = \pi d^2 h / 4$, typical $d = 100 \text{ µm}$, $h = 70 \text{ µm}$, $R \sim 1\text{–}10 \text{ mΩ}$. - **Self-alignment** in reflow driven by surface tension of molten solder — accuracy ±1–2 µm (vs. ±5–10 µm placement tolerance). - **CTE-mismatch shear strain:** $\gamma = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L / h$ — *largest at the corner bump* (max L). Typical values exceed the elastic limit → plastic deformation → fatigue. - **Coffin–Manson fatigue law:** $N_f = A \cdot (\Delta\gamma)^{-n}$ — used to predict thermal cycling life. - **Underfill** extends fatigue life by 10×–100× by constraining shear deformation. - **IMC layer** (Cu₆Sn₅, Cu₃Sn, Ag₃Sn) forms at the solder–pad interface; provides metallurgical bond but excessive growth is a reliability concern. - **Electromigration** safety limit: $J \lesssim 10^4 \text{ A/cm}^2$ for steady-state design. - **Hermeticity** for cavity packages: Au-Sn solder rings + fluxless reflow in N₂ atmosphere. - **RoHS compliance** has made lead-free SAC alloys the default — but *exempted* sectors (military, aerospace, implantable medical) still use high-Pb solders. - **Common reliability failures:** thermal fatigue (corner bump crack), electromigration voiding, IMC overgrowth, tin-whisker growth (Pb-free concern), pad cratering, Kirkendall voiding at the UBM–solder interface. --- > **End of Notes — Solder Alloy (PEICT632 / Module 4 / KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Bonding Process Using Solder Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bonding Process Using Solder Alloy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Solder Bonding** in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) is a *thermal fusion-based wafer-level or die-level packaging technique* in which a metallic filler material (solder alloy) is heated above its *melting temperature* (or eutectic temperature), allowed to wet the metallized surfaces of two substrates, and subsequently cooled to form a *hermetic, electrically conductive, and mechanically robust joint*. The process relies on *interfacial wetting*, *intermetallic compound (IMC) formation*, and *solidification* to achieve both mechanical interlocking and metallurgical bonding at the microscale.

In the context of KTU Module 4, solder bonding is classified under **intermediate-temperature bonding processes**, sitting between *low-temperature polymer/adhesive bonding* and *high-temperature fusion bonding* (such as silicon direct bonding or anodic bonding).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEICT632 Module 4):**
> Solder bonding is one of the most industrially deployed bonding processes for MEMS packaging because it simultaneously provides **electrical interconnection**, **hermetic sealing**, **thermal dissipation path**, and **mechanical support** in a single process step. KTU examiners frequently test this as a 14-mark question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of solder bonding like **"gluing two metal plates together using a low-melting metal paste instead of chemical glue."**

Imagine you are joining two copper water pipes in household plumbing. You do not weld the copper directly (that requires very high temperatures and skilled labour). Instead, you use a *tin-lead solder wire*. You heat the joint with a torch until the solder melts, it flows into the gap by capillary action, wets the copper surface, and when you remove the heat, it solidifies into a leak-proof, mechanically strong joint. **Solder bonding in MEMS is the miniaturized, cleanroom-compatible, wafer-scale version of exactly this process** — except the "torch" is a precision hot plate or rapid thermal annealer, the "pipes" are MEMS dice with micromachined cavities, and the "solder" is a precisely deposited thin-film alloy of gold-tin, gold-silicon, or tin-silver-copper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Solder Bonding Success:**
> 1. **Wetting** – molten solder must spread and adhere to the metallized pad.
> 2. **Wettable Metallurgy** – the bond pads must be coated with a *solder-wettable* metal (typically Au, Ni/Au, Cu, or Ag).
> 3. **Controlled Thermal Budget** – peak temperature must exceed the *liquidus* but stay below the *degradation threshold* of the MEMS device and CMOS circuitry.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Bonding Temperature** | **200 °C – 400 °C** | Above solder liquidus |
| **Bonding Force / Pressure** | **0.1 MPa – 5 MPa** | Ensures intimate contact |
| **Bonding Time (Dwell)** | **30 s – 10 min** | Time above liquidus |
| **Ambient Atmosphere** | **N₂ or Forming Gas (N₂/H₂)** | Prevents oxidation |
| **Bond Strength (Tensile)** | **20 MPa – 60 MPa** | Comparable to eutectic Au-Sn |
| **Hermeticity (He Leak Rate)** | **< 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s** | MIL-STD-883 compliant |
| **Joint Thickness** | **5 µm – 100 µm** | Set by solder pre-form or screen print |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle – Step-by-Step Logic

The solder bonding process unfolds in **five sequential physical stages**:

**Stage 1 — Surface Preparation & Metallization**
- Bond pads on both the MEMS wafer and the cap/handle wafer are deposited with a *solder-wettable* metal stack.
- Typical stack: **Ti (50 nm) / Ni (200 nm) / Au (100 nm)** — Ti acts as an adhesion layer, Ni as a diffusion barrier, and Au as the wettable, oxidation-resistant outer surface.

**Stage 2 — Solder Deposition**
- Solder material is placed on one or both bonding surfaces via:
  - **Electroplating** (most common for patterned MEMS)
  - **Screen / stencil printing** of solder paste
  - **Pre-form placement** (a precision-cut solder foil)
  - **Evaporation or sputtering** (for thin-film solders)
  - **Electroless deposition**

**Stage 3 — Alignment & Pre-Heating**
- Wafers are aligned (typically with ±1 µm to ±5 µm accuracy in a wafer bonder).
- The stack is pre-heated in a controlled atmosphere (**N₂** with < 10 ppm O₂) to remove moisture and outgas flux residues.

**Stage 4 — Reflow (The Heart of the Process)**
- Temperature is ramped above the solder *liquidus* (or *eutectic temperature*).
- **Wetting** occurs: molten solder spreads across the Au/Ni/Cu metallization.
- **Intermetallic Compound (IMC) formation** begins at the solder–metal interface. Examples:
  - Au-Sn system → **AuSn, AuSn₂, AuSn₄** phases
  - Sn-Ag-Cu system → **Ag₃Sn, Cu₆Sn₅** whiskers/plates
- A bonding force (0.1–5 MPa) is applied to expel voids and maintain a uniform joint thickness (governed by *stoppers* or *spacers* in the layout).

**Stage 5 — Cooling & Solidification**
- The assembly is cooled in a controlled ramp (typically 1–5 °C/s).
- Solder solidifies into a dense polycrystalline joint.
- IMC layers continue to grow during cooling, strengthening the metallurgical bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters for KTU:**
> Examiners love asking *"Why is Ni used as a diffusion barrier in solder bonding?"* The answer: Ni slows the diffusion of Cu (or Si) into the molten solder, preventing the formation of brittle, high-melting-point IMCs (e.g., Cu₃Sn, Cu₆Sn₅) that would otherwise deplete the solder volume and weaken the joint.

### 2.2 The Eutectic Phenomenon (Why Eutectic Solders Dominate)

A **eutectic alloy** has a *single, sharp melting point* (the eutectic temperature, **Tₑ**) rather than a melting *range*. At the eutectic composition, the liquid transforms directly into a two-phase solid mixture at **Tₑ** — this is ideal for bonding because:
- The entire joint melts and solidifies **isothermally**, minimizing thermal stress.
- The microstructure is fine and uniform (lamellar or fibrous), giving high mechanical strength.
- The process window is narrow and predictable — easy to control in production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Au-Sn system is the *gold standard* (literally) for MEMS hermetic packaging.** Its eutectic composition is **80 wt% Au / 20 wt% Sn** with **Tₑ = 280 °C**. This is low enough to be compatible with finished CMOS wafers (which typically tolerate ≤ 350 °C) and high enough to survive subsequent board-level reflow (peak ~ 260 °C for lead-free SAC).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Young's Equation (Wetting)** | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$ | Predicts contact angle $\theta$; good wetting if $\theta < 90°$ |
| 2 | **Work of Adhesion** | $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Energy released when solder wets a surface |
| 3 | **Spreading Coefficient** | $S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$ | If $S > 0$, solder spreads spontaneously |
| 4 | **Spontaneous IMC Growth (Arrhenius)** | $x(t) = x_0 + \sqrt{D_0 \, t \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)}$ | IMC layer thickness $x$ vs time $t$ and temperature $T$ |
| 5 | **Thermal Mismatch Strain** | $\varepsilon = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Stress at bond interface due to CTE mismatch $\Delta\alpha$ |
| 6 | **Lap Shear Strength Estimate** | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A} \approx 20\text{–}60 \,\text{MPa}$ (Au-Sn) | Quality check for bonded joint |
| 7 | **Eutectic Composition Rule** | $\dfrac{m_A}{M_A} \cdot x_A + \dfrac{m_B}{M_B} \cdot x_B = 1$ | Determines $x_B$ from $x_A$ in binary alloy |
| 8 | **Fick's First Law (Diffusion Barrier)** | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Justifies Ni as a barrier layer |
| 9 | **Hermeticity (He Leak Rate)** | $R = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\Delta t} < 10^{-8} \,\text{atm·cc/s}$ | MIL-STD-883 pass criterion |
| 10 | **Joint Thickness (Stopper-Set)** | $h_{joint} = h_{stopper}$ | Solder volume does *not* determine thickness; stopper does |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In the Young's equation, the *vertical pipe* symbol for absolute value must be rendered using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert \Delta\alpha \vert$) inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solder bonding is the **workhorse technology** behind:

- **Hermetic MEMS packages** for automotive sensors (pressure, accelerometer, gyroscope) — e.g., Bosch, STMicroelectronics, Infineon production lines.
- **RF MEMS switch and filter packaging** — where low-loss, hermetic Au-Sn seals preserve Q-factor.
- **Microbolometer and IR sensor arrays** — vacuum-sealed packages.
- **Optoelectronic device packaging** (VCSELs, photodiodes) — Au-Sn provides both electrical feedthrough and hermetic seal.
- **Power electronics die-attach** — SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu) lead-free solders for high-current devices.
- **3D IC stacking** — micro-bump solder joints (Cu-pillar + Sn-cap) in TSV-based 3D integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The RoHS (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) directive has pushed the industry from **eutectic Sn-Pb (63/37, Tₑ = 183 °C)** toward **lead-free SAC alloys (Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu, Tₑ = 217 °C)** for consumer electronics, but **Au-Sn remains the standard for high-reliability hermetic MEMS** because of its superior corrosion resistance and absence of whisker formation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young's Equation from Surface Energy Balance

We consider a sessile solder droplet resting on a flat, wettable substrate in thermodynamic equilibrium. Three interfacial tensions act on the *contact line* (the triple junction of solid, liquid, and vapour):

- $\gamma_{SV}$ — solid–vapour interfacial energy (per unit length, J/m)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ — solid–liquid interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{LV}$ — liquid–vapour (surface tension of molten solder)

**Step 1:** Resolve the horizontal components of these tensions acting on the contact line.

The liquid–vapour surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$ pulls along the liquid surface at an angle $\theta$ (the *contact angle*) measured through the liquid.

**Step 2:** Force balance along the substrate plane (per unit length of the contact line):

$$
\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to obtain the *cosine of the contact angle*:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}
$$

**Step 4:** This is **Young's Equation** (1805). It predicts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta < 90^\circ \;&\Longleftrightarrow\; \gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{SL} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Good wetting} \\
\theta = 0^\circ \;&\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Complete spreading (ideal bond)} \\
\theta > 90^\circ \;&\Longleftrightarrow\; \gamma_{SV} < \gamma_{SL} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{De-wetting (bond fails)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Define the *Work of Adhesion* $W_a$ — the energy per unit area required to separate the liquid solder from the solid:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}
$$

**Step 6:** Define the *Spreading Coefficient* $S$ — the free-energy change when the liquid spreads on the solid:

$$
S = W_a - 2\gamma_{LV} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}
$$

**Step 7:** Combining Steps 5, 6, and Young's equation:

$$
S = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1)
$$

Since $\cos\theta \le 1$, we have $S \le 0$. The system is in equilibrium when $S = 0$ (i.e., $\theta = 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for MEMS bonding:** If the metallized pad is contaminated or oxidized, $\gamma_{SL}$ rises, $\cos\theta$ becomes negative, and the molten solder balls up instead of spreading. This is why *in-situ flux* (rosin-based for Sn-Pb) or *fluxless vacuum/N₂ reflow* (for Au-Sn) is essential.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Intermetallic Compound (IMC) Growth Kinetics

IMC layers grow at the solder–metal interface via solid-state diffusion. The growth follows a **parabolic (diffusion-controlled) law**.

**Step 1:** Assume 1-D diffusion across a planar IMC layer of thickness $x(t)$.

**Step 2:** Fick's first law gives the flux $J$ of the diffusing species:

$$
J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 3:** The diffusivity $D$ is temperature-dependent (Arrhenius):

$$
D(T) = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is the activation energy (eV), $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Step 4:** For a diffusion-controlled process, the IMC thickness grows as the square root of time:

$$
x(t) = x_0 + \sqrt{D(T) \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the Arrhenius form of $D(T)$:

$$
\boxed{\,x(t) = x_0 + \sqrt{D_0 \, t \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)}\,}
$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation for KTU problems:
- Doubling the bonding time increases IMC thickness by only $\sqrt{2}$ (≈ 41%) — diminishing returns.
- Increasing the bonding temperature by 20 °C can *quadruple* the IMC growth rate near 280 °C.
- **Excessive IMC is detrimental** — the IMCs (e.g., AuSn₄) are *brittle* and consume the ductile solder, weakening the joint.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Mismatch Stress at the Bond Interface

When two materials with different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) are bonded and cooled, they contract by different amounts, inducing shear stress at the interface.

**Step 1:** Define the CTE mismatch:

$$
\Delta\alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2
$$

**Step 2:** The temperature drop from the *stress-free temperature* (≈ solidus of solder) to room temperature is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{solidus} - T_{room}
$$

**Step 3:** For a constrained, biaxially bonded layer, the in-plane strain is:

$$
\varepsilon = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 4:** The corresponding in-plane stress (assuming elastic behaviour, biaxial modulus $M$):

$$
\sigma = M \cdot \varepsilon = M \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where for an isotropic material $M = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu}$ (plane-strain modulus), with $E$ = Young's modulus and $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

**Step 5:** For a typical MEMS cap-to-silicon die joint, with $\Delta\alpha \approx 5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{K}^{-1}$ and $\Delta T \approx 250 \,\text{K}$:

$$
\varepsilon \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

This small strain is sufficient to *crack brittle IMCs* or *delaminate* poorly bonded interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Mitigation:** Use *compliant* solder joints (e.g., thick Au-Sn pre-forms), or *matched-CTE* substrates (e.g., Si-to-Si, or Si-to-AlN), or *stress-relief geometries* (e.g., corrugated bond frames).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Solder Alloys for MEMS

| Alloy | Eutectic / Liquidus $T$ (°C) | Composition | Key Properties | Typical MEMS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Au-Sn** | **280** | 80 Au / 20 Sn | Hermetic, no flux needed, no whiskers, high strength | Hermetic cap bonding (Bosch, STM) |
| **Au-Si** | **363** | 97.15 Au / 2.85 Si (at Au-rich eutectic) | Very high strength, Si-wettable | Si-to-Si wafer bonding |
| **Au-Ge** | **356** | 88 Au / 12 Ge | Lower cost than Au-Sn | Optoelectronic packaging |
| **Sn-Pb (eutectic)** | **183** | 63 Sn / 37 Pb | Lowest $T$, easy processing | Legacy / non-RoHS |
| **SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu)** | **217** | Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu | Lead-free, high strength, RoHS | Consumer MEMS, die-attach |
| **Sn-Ag** | **221** | Sn-3.5Ag | Lead-free, simple binary | Power die-attach |
| **In (pure)** | **157** | 99.99 In | Very low $T$, soft, ductile | Temperature-sensitive devices |
| **Bi-Sn** | **139** | 58 Bi / 42 Sn | Lowest practical $T$ | Step-soldering, thermal fuses |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
*Define solder bonding. List any two advantages of using Au-Sn eutectic alloy for MEMS packaging.*

**Model Answer:**

**Solder Bonding** is a thermal fusion-based packaging process in which a low-melting-point metallic alloy is heated above its liquidus temperature, wets pre-metallized bond pads, and solidifies to form a hermetic, mechanically robust, and electrically conductive joint between a MEMS die and its package cap (or another wafer).

**Two advantages of Au-Sn (80Au/20Sn, Tₑ = 280 °C):**
1. **Fluxless processing** — Au-Sn is oxidation-resistant and can be reflowed in a clean N₂ ambient without the need for chemical flux, eliminating flux-residue contamination inside the hermetic cavity (critical for optical and inertial MEMS).
2. **High reliability and no whisker formation** — Unlike pure Sn or SAC, Au-Sn forms stable, non-brittle IMCs and does not grow tin whiskers, ensuring long-term (≥ 10 years) hermeticity required by MIL-STD-883.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
*State Young's equation and explain the condition for good wetting in solder bonding.*

**Model Answer:**

**Young's Equation** (force balance at the solid–liquid–vapour triple line):

$$
\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta
$$

where $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$ are the solid–vapour, solid–liquid, and liquid–vapour interfacial energies, and $\theta$ is the *contact angle* measured through the liquid.

**Condition for good wetting:**

$$
\theta < 90^\circ \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{SL}
$$

When $\theta < 90°$, the molten solder spreads spontaneously on the metallized pad, ensuring complete joint formation. If the pad is contaminated, oxidized, or has poor wettability, $\theta > 90°$ and the solder de-wets, leading to bond failure (voids, poor hermeticity).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Question]**
*(a) [7 Marks]* Explain the step-by-step process of solder bonding in MEMS packaging. Discuss the role of each metallization layer (Ti, Ni, Au) in the bond-pad stack.

*(b) [7 Marks]* With a neat phase-diagram sketch, explain the eutectic behaviour of the Au-Sn system. Why is the eutectic composition (80Au/20Sn) preferred over off-eutectic compositions for MEMS hermetic packaging?

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Solder Bonding Process [7 Marks]**

| Step | Process | Purpose / Detail |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wafer Cleaning** (RCA SC-1, SC-2, DI rinse, spin-dry) | Remove organics, particles, native oxide; ensure a contamination-free surface for metallization. |
| 2 | **Bond-Pad Metallization** (e-beam evaporation or sputtering) | Deposit Ti / Ni / Au stack (typical: Ti 50 nm / Ni 200 nm / Au 100 nm). |
| 3 | **Solder Deposition** (electro-plating, screen printing, or pre-form) | Place the Au-Sn alloy in a *ring* or *frame* geometry enclosing the MEMS active area. |
| 4 | **Wafer Alignment** (mask aligner / wafer bonder) | Align cap wafer to MEMS wafer with ±1–5 µm accuracy; bring into contact. |
| 5 | **Atmosphere Control** (vacuum + N₂ purge) | O₂ < 10 ppm to prevent oxidation of molten solder and MEMS surfaces. |
| 6 | **Pre-Heating** (≈ 150 °C, 5 min) | Drive out moisture and adsorbed gases; thermal equilibration. |
| 7 | **Reflow** (T > 280 °C + 20–40 °C superheat, 30 s – 10 min) | Solder melts, wets Au, forms IMCs (AuSn, AuSn₂). |
| 8 | **Cooling** (1–5 °C/s ramp) | Controlled solidification → fine-grained joint; *external* stoppers set joint thickness. |
| 9 | **Inspection** (X-ray, SAM, He leak test) | Verify hermeticity, void-free joint, bond strength. |

**Role of each metallization layer:**

- **Ti (50 nm) — Adhesion Layer:**
  Ti forms a strong chemical bond with the SiO₂ or Si₃N₄ passivation on the substrate. It has a high affinity for oxygen, anchoring the stack to the dielectric. Without Ti, the upper layers would *peel off* under thermal stress.

- **Ni (200 nm) — Diffusion Barrier:**
  Ni prevents Cu (from interconnects) and Si (from the substrate) from diffusing into the molten Au-Sn during reflow. Such diffusion would form *brittle* Cu-Sn or Ni-Sn IMCs, consume the solder, and degrade joint strength. Ni also slows Au diffusion, controlling the *rate* of Au-Sn IMC growth.

- **Au (100 nm) — Wettable, Oxidation-Resistant Outer Layer:**
  Au is virtually inert at room temperature, so the bond pad remains *wettable* even after extended storage. Molten Sn-Au readily dissolves Au to form Au-Sn IMCs, producing a strong metallurgical bond. The 100 nm Au also acts as a *sacrificial layer* — it dissolves entirely into the solder, leaving a clean Ni-Sn interface.

**(b) Eutectic Behaviour of the Au-Sn System [7 Marks]**

The Au-Sn binary phase diagram features a *eutectic point* at:

- **Composition:** 80 wt% Au / 20 wt% Sn
- **Eutectic Temperature (Tₑ):** 280 °C
- **Eutectic Phases:** AuSn (intermetallic, ζ phase) + Au-rich solid solution (β phase)

```
Temperature (°C)
       │  \         Liquidus /
   400 │   \       /         \
       │    \     /           \
   300 │     \   /  Au5Sn (ζ′)  \      ← ζ + β eutectic line
   Tₑ=280├───●─────────────────────── (Eutectic point: 80Au/20Sn)
   200 │    /│\ 
       │   / │ \  Two-phase:
   100 │  /  │  \ AuSn (ζ) + Au(β)
       │ /   │   \
     0 └────┴────┴──────► Au%     Sn%
        0  20  40   60   80  100
```

**Why the eutectic composition is preferred:**

1. **Sharp, single melting point (280 °C).** Off-eutectic compositions melt over a *temperature range* (e.g., 90Au/10Sn pastes melt over 217–280 °C). During the range, a *partially molten* state exists — the solid phase obstructs flow, traps voids, and produces non-uniform joints. The eutectic melts *isothermally*, giving clean, complete reflow.

2. **Fine, uniform eutectic microstructure.** At Tₑ, the liquid transforms directly into an intimate mixture of AuSn and Au-rich phases, producing a fine lamellar structure with high mechanical strength and good ductility.

3. **No liquidus + solidus gap.** The bonding process window is narrow and predictable, enabling high throughput and low defect rates in production.

4. **Hermetic, void-free joints.** The isothermal melt and uniform solidification minimize shrinkage voids, which is critical for hermetic packaging (He leak rate < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s).

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Question]**
*(a) [7 Marks]* Derive the intermetallic compound (IMC) growth equation for a solder joint. Discuss the effect of bonding temperature and time on IMC thickness and joint reliability.

*(b) [7 Marks]* Compare the Au-Sn, Au-Si, and Sn-Pb eutectic solders in terms of (i) eutectic temperature, (ii) flux requirement, (iii) hermeticity, (iv) cost, and (v) typical MEMS application.

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of IMC Growth Equation [7 Marks]**

Intermetallic compounds grow at the solder–metal interface by *solid-state diffusion* of one or both species (e.g., Au atoms diffuse into molten Sn during reflow; later, Au and Sn inter-diffuse in the solid joint during service).

**Step 1 — Diffusion flux (Fick's First Law):**

For a planar IMC layer of thickness $x$ with concentration drop $\Delta C$ across it, the steady-state flux of the diffusing species (per unit area) is:

$$
J = -D \, \frac{\Delta C}{x} = \frac{D \, \Delta C}{x}
$$

(Negative sign indicates flux direction down the concentration gradient; we use magnitude for clarity.)

**Step 2 — Mass balance at the interface:**

The IMC grows by incorporating atoms at both its boundaries. The growth rate $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ is proportional to the flux:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \Omega \cdot J = \frac{\Omega \, D \, \Delta C}{x}
$$

where $\Omega$ is a constant with units of m³/atom (converts atomic flux to linear growth rate).

**Step 3 — Integration:**

Separating variables and integrating from $t = 0$ (where $x = x_0$, the initial IMC thickness, often ≈ 0) to time $t$:

$$
\int_{x_0}^{x(t)} x \, dx = \Omega \, D \, \Delta C \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\frac{x^2 - x_0^2}{2} = \Omega \, D \, \Delta C \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x(t)$:**

$$
x(t) = \sqrt{x_0^2 + 2 \, \Omega \, D \, \Delta C \cdot t}
$$

For $x_0 \approx 0$:

$$
\boxed{\,x(t) \approx \sqrt{2 \, \Omega \, D \, \Delta C \cdot t} \;\propto\; \sqrt{D \, t}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Arrhenius temperature dependence:**

The diffusivity $D$ depends on temperature through the Arrhenius relation:

$$
D(T) = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,x(t) = \sqrt{2 \, \Omega \, \Delta C \, D_0 \, t \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation and reliability impact:**

- The IMC thickness grows as the **square root of time** — a *parabolic* law.
- IMC growth is **exponentially sensitive to temperature** — a 20 °C rise near 280 °C can *double* the growth rate.
- *Excessive* IMC is harmful: the IMCs (e.g., AuSn₄, Ag₃Sn) are **brittle** and consume the ductile, fatigue-resistant β-Sn matrix, leading to:
  - Loss of ductility and impact resistance
  - Initiation of micro-cracks at the IMC–solder interface
  - Depletion of the compliant solder layer, transferring stress to the rigid IMC
- *Optimal* IMC thickness: typically **1–3 µm** of AuSn / AuSn₂ at the bond interface gives the best balance of strength and ductility.
- **Process rule:** Keep *both* bonding time and temperature at the lower end of the acceptable window, while ensuring complete reflow and wetting. Avoid prolonged high-temperature storage.

**(b) Comparison of Au-Sn, Au-Si, and Sn-Pb Eutectic Solders [7 Marks]**

| Property | **Au-Sn (80/20)** | **Au-Si (97.15/2.85)** | **Sn-Pb (63/37)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Eutectic Temperature** | **280 °C** | **363 °C** | **183 °C** |
| (ii) **Flux Requirement** | **No flux needed** (fluxless in N₂) | No flux needed | **Flux required** (rosin-based) |
| (iii) **Hermeticity** | **Excellent** (no voids, no whiskers) | Excellent | Moderate (flux residue may outgas) |
| (iv) **Cost** | **Very High** (Au content) | High (Au + Si) | **Low** |
| (v) **Typical MEMS Application** | Hermetic cap bonding (inertial, optical, RF MEMS) | Si-to-Si wafer bonding (high-T tolerant) | Legacy consumer MEMS, low-cost die-attach |

**Detailed discussion:**

- **Au-Sn (Tₑ = 280 °C):** The industry standard for high-reliability hermetic MEMS. The high Au content makes it expensive, but the fluxless reflow, absence of tin whiskers, and excellent hermeticity justify the cost in automotive, medical, and aerospace applications.

- **Au-Si (Tₑ = 363 °C):** Used where very high joint strength is required and the device can tolerate a high bonding temperature. The silicon in the eutectic alloy is *wettable* on bare Si wafers, making Au-Si attractive for Si-to-Si direct-style bonding with a metallic seal.

- **Sn-Pb (Tₑ = 183 °C):** Historically the *universal* solder. The low temperature protects temperature-sensitive devices, and the alloy is cheap and easy to process. However, **Pb is banned under RoHS** for consumer electronics, and Sn-Pb joints grow tin whiskers over time, which can cause short circuits. Today, Sn-Pb is restricted to **aerospace, military, and high-reliability** applications exempted from RoHS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not write "Au-Sn melting point is 280 °C"** without specifying the composition. Always state **"80Au/20Sn eutectic alloy, Tₑ = 280 °C"** — examiners deduct ½ mark for missing the composition.
> 2. **Do not confuse "liquidus" and "solidus"** with "eutectic temperature." For a *pure eutectic* alloy, liquidus = solidus = Tₑ. Off-eutectic alloys have a *range* (liquidus ≠ solidus).
> 3. **In derivation questions, show every integration step.** A common error is to write $x(t) = \sqrt{Dt}$ without the dimensional constant, the initial thickness $x_0$, or the integration. Examiners expect a clear separation of variables and the limits of integration.
> 4. **Avoid stating "Au-Sn is the best" without justification.** Always back up superlatives with a property (e.g., "Au-Sn is *preferred for hermetic packaging* because it does not require flux and does not form tin whiskers").
> 5. **Do not forget the role of stoppers/spacers** when explaining joint thickness control — a common omission. The solder volume does *not* determine the final joint thickness; *mechanical stoppers* (or seal-ring geometry) do.
> 6. **In phase-diagram sketches, always label both axes** (composition on x-axis, temperature on y-axis) and *mark* the eutectic point with its Tₑ and composition. Unlabelled diagrams lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Solder Bonding (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Solder bonding is a thermal fusion process using a low-melting metal alloy to form hermetic, conductive, mechanically strong joints.
- **Most-used alloy in MEMS:** **Au-Sn (80Au/20Sn, Tₑ = 280 °C)** — fluxless, hermetic, no whiskers.
- **Standard bond-pad stack:** **Ti (50 nm) / Ni (200 nm) / Au (100 nm)** — adhesion / barrier / wettable.
- **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$. **Good wetting** requires $\theta < 90°$.
- **Spreading Coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) \le 0$. Spontaneous spreading if $S \to 0$.
- **IMC Growth Law:** $x(t) = \sqrt{D_0 \, t \, \exp(-E_a / k_B T)}$ — parabolic in time, Arrhenius in temperature.
- **Optimal IMC thickness:** **1–3 µm** of AuSn / AuSn₂. *Thicker* is *worse* (brittle).
- **Process Window:** Reflow at Tₑ + 20–40 °C, dwell 30 s – 10 min, force 0.1–5 MPa, atmosphere N₂ (O₂ < 10 ppm).
- **Hermeticity Criterion:** He leak rate **< 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s** (per MIL-STD-883).
- **Joint thickness control:** Set by **mechanical stoppers**, *not* by solder volume.
- **Thermal mismatch strain:** $\varepsilon = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ — drives stress at the interface; mitigated by compliant joints or matched CTE.
- **Eutectic vs. off-eutectic:** Eutectic → single sharp Tₑ, isothermal melt, fine microstructure, preferred for bonding.
- **RoHS trend:** Sn-Pb (183 °C) is being replaced by **SAC (Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu, 217 °C)** in consumer products; **Au-Sn remains the gold standard for high-reliability hermetic MEMS**.
- **Comparison anchors to memorize:**
  - Lowest $Tₑ$ → **Bi-Sn (139 °C)**, then **In (157 °C)**, then **Sn-Pb (183 °C)**, then **SAC (217 °C)**, then **Au-Sn (280 °C)**, then **Au-Ge (356 °C)**, then **Au-Si (363 °C)**.
  - *Always* remember: **Au-Sn = 280 °C = 80Au/20Sn = fluxless = hermetic = no whiskers = expensive**.
- **Diffusion barrier logic:** Ni is the barrier of choice because it slows Cu and Si diffusion into the molten solder, preventing *brittle* ternary IMCs.
- **Why flux is avoided in Au-Sn bonding:** Flux residue inside a hermetic cavity can *outgas* over time, degrading vacuum and contaminating optical / inertial surfaces.
- **Process steps to recite in order:** Clean → Metallize (Ti/Ni/Au) → Deposit solder → Align → Pump & purge N₂ → Pre-heat → Reflow → Cool → Inspect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections** in MEMS (Micro Electro Mechanical Systems) refer to a class of **lead-free, polymer-matrix based electrical and mechanical joining technologies** used to establish signal, power, and ground paths between a MEMS die/chip and its substrate, package, or external circuitry. The wiring medium is a **thermosetting or thermoplastic organic resin (epoxy, acrylic, silicone, or polyimide)** loaded with **conductive fillers** — typically metallic particles (Ni, Ag, Au, Cu), metallic-coated polymer spheres, or carbon-based nanomaterials — which, when subjected to a controlled thermocompression cycle, produce either **anisotropic** (direction-dependent) or **isotropic** (direction-independent) conductivity across the bond line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** Organic adhesive bonding is positioned as a **low-temperature, low-stress, fine-pitch alternative to solder reflow**, particularly suited to MEMS because many microstructures (cantilevers, membranes, proof masses) cannot survive the **>250 °C** peak temperatures of conventional Sn-Pb or SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu) soldering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are joining two LEGO plates that have tiny metallic nubs (the "bumps") on their surfaces:

- **Soldering** is like melting a metal paste (solder) in an oven at very high temperature. It works, but the heat can warp delicate MEMS membranes.
- **Organic Adhesive Bonding** is like spreading a special **"smart glue"** between the plates that contains microscopic metal-coated balls. When you press and slightly heat the assembly, the glue hardens *and* the metal balls get squashed between the contact pads, creating an electrical path — **without melting anything**.

There are three variants of this "smart glue":

| Glue Type | Behaviour | Analogy |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Anisotropic Conductive Adhesive (ACA / ACF)** | Conducts only in **Z-direction** (vertical) | A sponge with vertical pins — sideways stays insulating |
| **Isotropic Conductive Adhesive (ICA)** | Conducts in **all directions** | A metal-filed dough — conductive everywhere |
| **Non-Conductive Adhesive (NCA)** | Insulating; relies on direct pad-to-particle contact | Pure glue — only direct metal contact conducts |

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Process Metrics used in KTU Board Problems:**
> - **Bonding Temperature (T<sub>b</sub>):** 150 °C – 220 °C (vs. 350 °C for solder)
> - **Bonding Pressure (P<sub>b</sub>):** 1 MPa – 50 MPa (typical: **10–30 MPa**)
> - **Bonding Time (t<sub>b</sub>):** 5 s – 30 s
> - **Filler Particle Diameter:** 3 μm – 10 μm (Ni, Ag-coated polymer)
> - **ACF Film Thickness:** 25 μm – 50 μm
> - **Pitch Capability:** down to **20 μm** (vs. 100 μm for solder)
> - **Volume Resistivity (cured):** 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ Ω·cm for ICA; 10⁻³ Ω·cm joint for ACA

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
